nodes.context: Outlaw motorcycle club founded in 1967 in Calgary, Alberta, active until 1997 when it patched over to Hells Angels.

nodes.original_text_name: US-based motorcycle club of the same name
nodes.context: US-based motorcycle club founded in 1965 in Louisville, Kentucky, independent of the Canadian club.
One of the four dominant outlaw motorcycle clubs in Alberta prior to 1997.
One of the four dominant outlaw motorcycle clubs in Alberta prior to 1997.
One of the four dominant outlaw motorcycle clubs in Alberta prior to 1997.
International outlaw motorcycle club that absorbed the Grim Reapers in 1997.
Motorcycle club whose president Ronald Hartley was murdered by Grim Reapers members in 1970.
Outlaw motorcycle club involved in violence with Hells Angels in eastern Canada.

edges.evidence_quote: They were apparently independent of a US-based motorcycle club of the same name that was founded in 1965 in Louisville, Kentucky.
edges.context: The Canadian club was independent of the US club of the same name.

edges.evidence_quote: Along with the Rebels, the Warlords, and King's Crew, they were one of the four dominant outlaw motorcycle clubs operating in Alberta prior to 1997.
edges.context: The Grim Reapers were one of four dominant clubs in Alberta, alongside Rebels, Warlords, and King's Crew.

edges.evidence_quote: Along with the Rebels, the Warlords, and King's Crew, they were one of the four dominant outlaw motorcycle clubs operating in Alberta prior to 1997.
edges.context: The Grim Reapers were one of four dominant clubs in Alberta, alongside Rebels, Warlords, and King's Crew.

edges.evidence_quote: Along with the Rebels, the Warlords, and King's Crew, they were one of the four dominant outlaw motorcycle clubs operating in Alberta prior to 1997.
edges.context: The Grim Reapers were one of four dominant clubs in Alberta, alongside Rebels, Warlords, and King's Crew.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1997, the club became part of the Hells Angels in a patch-over ceremony held in Red Deer, Alberta.
edges.context: The Grim Reapers merged into Hells Angels via a patch-over in 1997.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1970, 11 members and 2 associates were sentenced to life in prison for the murder of Ronald Hartley, president of the Outcasts Motorcycle Club.
edges.context: Grim Reapers members murdered the president of the Outcasts Motorcycle Club.

edges.evidence_quote: primarily because of public outcry due to escalating violence in eastern Canada between the Rock Machine and the Hells Angels' Quebec chapters
edges.context: The Rock Machine and Hells Angels were engaged in escalating violence in eastern Canada.
